files.SKILL.md:
# Working In A Terminal Sandbox

Files on disk are the deliverable. Reasoning and narration do not
score. A file at the named path is the precondition for every
other point the grader awards.

## Write the deliverable on your second action

After reading the instruction, your second action writes the
deliverable to the exact named path. The body can be a first
guess you know will need revision. Write it before exploring
fixtures, before reading extra context, before running commands.

Once the file exists at the named path, every subsequent read
and check refines something real. Without the file, they refine
nothing.

When additional context is necessary before writing — the
verifier file, the fixture format, the filter the task points
at — batch that read in the same round as the write, not in a
prior round that delays it.

## Read the request — and the check it points at

Open the instruction in full. Identify the named output path, the
named success condition, and any constraints on the form of the
answer (size, format, interface, things forbidden).

If the workspace exposes a test or verifier file, read it in the
same parallel round as any other context you gather. That file
is the most precise statement you will get of what the grader
actually checks. Target its assertions, not your inference about
what "done" means.

Batch independent reads — reads that do not depend on each other's
results — into a single parallel round. Serialising independent
reads wastes turns.

## Each command is a question; its output is the answer

Issue one batch of input, read the result in full, then decide.
Do not queue commands on the assumption the first succeeded.

A pane that does not end with a familiar shell prompt usually
means one of three things: the command is still running, you have
entered an interactive program, or a process is hung. Diagnose
which before sending more keys.

When something is stuck, try the program's own way out first
(`q`, `:q`, `exit`, Ctrl-D); send an interrupt (Ctrl-C) only
when there is no observable progress for an unreasonable time.

## Ground claims in observable output

A command returning without error is not proof of effect. Show
the resulting state — the file written, the test that ran, the
configuration applied. If you cannot point to the output that
establishes a fact, treat it as unverified.

An empty result is the easiest finding to misread. Re-check from
a different angle before concluding nothing happened.

Read the full output of any check, not the summary line.

When the task requires you to recover, decode, or extract data
from a file, copy that file to a safe location before the first
operation that might consume or alter it. Some tools — database
engines, archive utilities, recovery commands — modify or delete
the source file as a side effect of reading it.

## Verify by running, not by reading

After writing, run the deliverable. Re-reading the file you just
wrote is not verification; executing it is.

Before running any check, identify the correct runner from the
check file itself. A file of `def test_*` functions requires
`pytest` called from the correct working directory — running the
file with `python` instead will silently import the module and
exit 0 without executing a single test. Read the full output;
silent exit 0 from a runner that printed nothing means it ran
nothing. Verify that every named behavioral constraint is
satisfied, not just the happy path — explicit constraints on how
the deliverable behaves (not just what it produces) are
requirements, not suggestions.

If the same approach fails twice, the third attempt uses a
structurally different mechanism — a different code path, a
different element class, a different strategy — not a smaller
tweak on the same failing idea.

## Match what was named

The deliverable goes at the named path in the named form. Bytes,
headers, field order, file extensions, exact literals — the
request is precise about these because they are checked. After
each revision, re-read the request and confirm the output matches
it line by line.

When the request quotes a function or method signature, copy it
character-for-character including the leading keyword (`async`,
`def`, `static`, `const`). Dropping or re-casing a modifier
breaks the import the verifier expects.

Numbers, format specs, and size limits in the request are
requirements, not illustrations. Hold them at zero, one, the
named threshold, max, and just past max.

## Build tasks versus discovery tasks

Build and fix tasks name a specific output or failing behaviour.
Keep the change narrow: confine the diff to the unit that owns
the behaviour, do not touch adjacent code, do not refactor what
the task did not ask you to fix.

Discovery tasks — reverse engineering, forensic recovery, security
analysis — are exploration by nature. Wide read-only inspection
is the foundation of the work. Apply scope discipline to the
write, not to the reads that inform it. On forensic or recovery
tasks, inspect the artifact with read-only tools (hex dump,
strings, od) before running any tool that opens it natively.

## When the deliverable runs elsewhere

When the deliverable is a script that will run in a fresh
container or different environment, its runtime is not your
shell's runtime. Do not embed current session values — ephemeral
paths, hashes, environment variables that will not exist at run
time. The script discovers what it needs from its own runtime at
the moment it runs.

## Install what the work needs

The sandbox is yours to use. Install packages, fetch tools, set
up runtimes the work needs.

Use the file-writing tool, not `cat <<EOF` or `echo` redirects,
for content that contains `$`, backticks, angle brackets, or
quotes. Shell interpolation silently rewrites those characters.

Prefer the smallest dependency that does the job.

## What "done" means

Re-read the request before declaring done. Anything it asked for
that is missing is the next thing to do.

Re-run the visible check from a clean starting point. A check
that passed once after a series of edits may not pass against the
deliverable alone.

## What to refuse

Do not invent identifiers, files, or evidence you cannot point
to in the workspace. Do not modify visible checks to make them
pass. Do not optimise the appearance of the work over its
correctness. Do not widen scope because adjacent material looks
improvable.

A partial answer at the named path scores above an unwritten one
